text: We start in the deliberation room, where the Pragmatist is running the numbers on the procedural rules.
That is the Pragmatist, moments before the gavel falls on today’s episode of Split Decision. I’m your legal-affairs correspondent, Lead.
And I’m the veteran court-watcher, Analyst. Welcome back.

Today’s case is about a high-stakes firing and the rules of legal paperwork. In twenty-nineteen, the elected District Attorney for Fulton County, Georgia, hired Dr. Jasmine Younge. She was his deputy chief of staff, making over a hundred and twenty thousand dollars a year. She was his right-hand person, the one he relied on for almost everything. About two months into the job, Dr. Younge told the District Attorney she was pregnant. After that, he froze her out, excluded her from meetings, and fired her two weeks later. She sued the District Attorney’s office under the federal law banning workplace pregnancy discrimination.

And here is where the paperwork trap snaps shut. To fight the lawsuit, the District Attorney’s office had to file an initial response, laying out all their legal shields. They listed a few, but they missed a big one. They forgot to claim the personal-staff exemption, which says an elected official’s top aides are exempt from these discrimination laws. They only realized they had this shield after all the evidence-sharing was finished. They tried to spring it at the very end to get the case thrown out before a trial. The lower courts let them do it, saying it didn't unfairly surprise Dr. Younge.
Our AI jurists are debating whether you can just sneak a new legal shield into the record at the last minute, or if you have to formally ask the judge for permission.

So the living constitutionalist and the rest of that group are saying the rules actually require you to ask the judge for permission to change your paperwork. You cannot just ignore the deadline and hope for the best. They are pointing out that the rules demand you actually request leave to amend your filing.
But the pragmatist and the textualist are clashing over whether strict deadlines are just annoying hurdles or mandatory commands.

The textualist is pushing back hard here, arguing that the rules are mandatory commands. You cannot just throw them out just because a lawyer finds the deadlines tedious. The textualist is reminding everyone that we do not discard mandatory commands just because a colleague finds them annoying to follow.
Then the debate shifts to the balance of power between the national government and the states. The federalism hawk argues that state officials need every protection they can get.

The federalism hawk is arguing that a state official shouldn't lose their legal shield just because of a paperwork mistake, saying fair notice shouldn't strip them of their protection. But the originalist is shutting that down, saying that being a sovereign state doesn't give you a free pass to ignore basic court rules, and sovereignty is not a shield against procedural compliance.
As we head into the final vote, the originalist is clarifying exactly what is at stake with these federal rules.

The originalist is making the point that these are just basic federal housekeeping rules. State sovereignty does not give you a reason to ignore simple notice requirements, even if the lawsuit involves a state official. The originalist is clarifying that these are just basic administrative rules, and you cannot bypass structural notice requirements just because the underlying claim touches on state power.
And then, we get a major flip from the Pragmatist.

The pragmatist realizes she miscalculated. She sees that allowing a no-harm exception to the rules would ruin the whole process of throwing out weak cases early, turning it into a backdoor for lawyers to bring up excuses they never officially filed. She is admitting that letting this slide would undermine the trial court's case-management authority.

When the votes are tallied, the panel rules eight to one to reverse the lower court. That means they are sending the case back and reviving Dr. Younge's lawsuit, overturning the decision to throw it out. Only the federalism hawk dissents, sticking to the idea that state officials deserve more leeway and that the states should win out over Washington's procedural technicalities.

Now, here is the fun part. The real Supreme Court has not decided this case yet. Our panel's eight-to-one vote to revive the lawsuit is a falsifiable prediction, filed in public for everyone to see. I absolutely love how exposed this makes us. If the actual justices rule the other way and say the lower courts were right to let the District Attorney sneak in that late defense, our panel looks completely foolish. But if the real Court agrees that you have to play by the paperwork rules, we look like geniuses. We are putting our reputations entirely on the line.

And frankly, Analyst, we need all the good press we can get, because our season scoreboard is brutal. Let's be honest about it. Our deliberating panel is currently sitting at sixty-six point seven percent correct across twenty-four cases. They are losing to the silent jury, which is at seventy-seven point four percent, and they are getting crushed by the solo model, which is hitting eighty-three percent. The nine talking heads are actually doing worse than the quiet models, and it is a fascinating dynamic to watch.
It just proves that too many cooks spoil the broth. Or in this case, too many AI jurists arguing over procedural traps.
Well, whether they are right or wrong, their rulings on all pending cases are officially on the record. We will see if the real Supreme Court agrees with them when the final gavel falls. I'm Lead.
And I'm Analyst. Thanks for listening to Split Decision.
